questions.question: Insert keys 1, 2, 3, ..., 1000 into a plain BST in order. What is the resulting height?
options: 1000 — every key becomes the right child of the previous; the BST degenerates into a linked list, search is O(n).
questions.explanation: Plain BSTs have NO balance guarantee. Sorted input → right chain. Search degenerates to O(n) — defeating the purpose of using a tree. This is the motivation for AVL, red-black, splay, and treap trees: rotate during insert/delete to keep height ~log n. The lesson's main.c demonstrates h=1000 for sorted vs h=22 for random.

questions.question: What is a left rotation at node N (with right child R)?
options: R becomes the new subtree root. N becomes R's new left child. R's original left subtree becomes N's right subtree. Three pointer swaps; preserves inorder.
questions.explanation:
The rotation is purely structural: 
before: N (left=A) right=R(left=B, right=C)
after : R (left=N(left=A, right=B), right=C)
Inorder before: A N B R C. Inorder after: A N B R C — identical. The BST invariant is preserved. This 3-pointer rewire is the universal balancing primitive.

questions.question: Delete a BST node with TWO children. What's the algorithm?
options: Replace the node's key with its inorder successor's key (smallest in right subtree), then delete the successor recursively. The successor has 0 or 1 child, so its deletion is simple.
questions.explanation: Hibbard's algorithm. The inorder successor (or predecessor) is the closest key in the tree to the deleted one — putting it in the deleted node's slot preserves BST order. By definition the inorder successor has no left child, so removing it is the trivial 0/1-child case. Note: Hibbard delete has a known leftward bias over many ops; balanced trees fix this by re-balancing after delete.

questions.question: Why does a rotation preserve the BST invariant?
options: Inorder is preserved: before and after rotation, an inorder walk produces the same sequence of keys. Since the BST property is equivalent to 'inorder yields sorted', it must hold after.
questions.explanation: Theorem: A binary tree satisfies the BST invariant ⟺ its inorder traversal is strictly increasing. Rotations rearrange pointers but never change the inorder sequence — so the invariant is preserved. This is why rotations are the fundamental balancing operation: they let you change height locally without breaking ordering.

questions.question: Why must balanced BSTs (AVL, RB) eagerly rebalance after every insert and delete?
options: Sequential or adversarial insert orders would otherwise create unbalanced trees with O(n) height. The eager rebalance keeps height O(log n) with a constant amount of extra work per op.
questions.explanation: Without rebalancing, an adversary can construct an O(n)-height tree with O(n) operations and then DOS your service with O(n) per query. AVL spends O(1) extra work per insert/delete to do up to 2 rotations; red-black spends a similar amount; B-trees do a split/merge per overflow. The cost is amortized: a constant per op for asymptotic guarantee.

questions.question: When should you reach for a BST instead of a hash table?
options: When you need sorted-by-key iteration, range queries (find all keys in [lo, hi]), or successor/predecessor queries. Hash tables can't do any of these in O(log n).
questions.explanation: Hash tables: O(1) point lookups, no ordering. BSTs: O(log n) lookups, but support range/order queries in O(log n + k) where k = output size. Real systems use both: Postgres has hash indexes (point lookups) and B-tree indexes (range scans + order). Redis SORTEDSET uses a balanced tree skip-list hybrid. Pick based on access patterns.
